frameworks.name: attractor dynamics
frameworks.one_line: Task-evoked activity increases stability around a stable attractor

frameworks.name: noise correlations
frameworks.one_line: Pairwise neural correlations and shared variability as information-coding constraints

frameworks.name: functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: Large-scale FC view that task states increase inter-region correlations

frameworks.name: neural mass / spiking computational models
frameworks.one_line: Models reproduce task-evoked quenching as attractor stabilization

core_question: Do co-active cortical regions during task states increase or decrease their correlations and variability?
key_assumptions: Reduced shared variability enhances information coding
Spiking and fMRI data probe a comparable correlation phenomenon
Dimensionality indexes information redundancy across regions
Stable attractors capture task-state cortical dynamics
manipulations: Two distinct data modalities: NHP spiking and human fMRI
Spiking and neural mass computational models contrasted
key_findings: Variability and correlations primarily decrease across cortical regions during task states
Reduction observed in both NHP spiking and human fMRI data
Dimensionality increases during task states, indicating less redundancy
Models show task-evoked activity increases stability around a stable attractor
interpretations: Decreased correlations expand information coding capacity during tasks
Task-evoked input quenches variability by deepening attractor stability globally
